interactions.summary: Asked which God-belief best controls society and why Jesus was killed, Jiang compares polytheism, monotheism, and no-God systems, then argues Jesus threatened the status quo by telling slaves and the oppressed they were divine souls.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Interview exchange: SPEAKER_00 is host Jack Neel or ad read; SPEAKER_01 is Jiang unless noted.

speaker_notes.note: Diarization is consistent enough for this packet: SPEAKER_00 carries host questions, intro, outro, or sponsor reads; SPEAKER_01 carries Jiang answers. Sponsor ad segments are not Jiang claims.

claims.claim: Jiang says polytheism encourages creative questioning, monotheism drives anxious energetic action and suffering, and no-God systems risk humans or emperors becoming godlike tyrants.
Jiang argues Jesus was likely crucified because his divine-soul message undermined Roman and Jewish authority by psychologically liberating slaves and the oppressed, creating economic damage.

signature_moments.moment: One God creates skyscrapers and high-speed rail but forgets to ask why.
signature_moments.source_phrase: no one ever asked the question why are we doing this
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It captures his critique of monotheistic energy without purpose.

signature_moments.moment: A slave who knows he is a divine soul becomes an economic threat.
signature_moments.source_phrase: you're a divine soul, you shouldn't be a slave
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This turns theology into material rebellion.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: A sayings collection Jiang treats as possibly early and aligned with a Gnostic divine-spark Jesus.
Worldview in which death is release and humans are divine sparks seeking knowledge beyond material slavery.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang dates this religious-political interpretation to the 2026 interview; no direct prior-position update is supplied.
